original_hypothesis.title: Missing Access Control on initiateClose Enables Protocol-Wide Griefing DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: initiateClose() has no msg.sender check — any external address can call it on any active channel. This forces the channel into an irreversible 1-hour grace period countdown, after which anyone can call finalize() to close the channel and refund unsettled deposits to the payer. An automated bot can continuously grief all active channels at minimal gas cost.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function lacks any authentication. Combined with finalize() also being callable by anyone, an attacker can force-close every channel in the protocol with just gas costs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initiateClose has no msg.sender validation; no require(msg.sender == channel.payer || msg.sender == channel.payee)

preconditions: Active (non-finalized) channels exist in the protocol
Attacker knows channelIds (observable from ChannelOpened events)
Attacker has gas to send transactions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No access control on state-changing function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any address can force any channel into irreversible close process
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No msg.sender check against channel.payer or channel.payee

analysis_questions: Can the grace period be reset or cancelled once initiated?
What is the financial impact if payee cannot settle all vouchers within 1 hour?
Can initiateClose be called repeatedly or is it one-time per channel?
validation_requirements: Confirm any address can call initiateClose on another user's channel
Verify gracePeriodEnd cannot be reset to 0 once set
Test that finalize succeeds after grace period from any caller

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Attacker monitors ChannelOpened events, collects all active channelIds. 2. Attacker calls initiateClose(channelId) for each active channel. 3. All channels enter 1-hour grace period. 4. Payees must scramble to settle all pending vouchers within 1 hour. 5. After 1 hour, attacker calls finalize() on each channel. 6. Unsettled amounts are refunded to payers, payees lose earned payments.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All unsettled voucher value across the protocol at risk of loss to payees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All active payees in the protocol
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Payees can mitigate by settling vouchers within grace period, but automated attack on all channels simultaneously may overwhelm

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for third-party calling initiateClose

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~50K gas per initiateClose call; scalable to many channels
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-transaction: initiateClose then finalize after 1 hour

validation_result.impact_summary: Any address can force any active payment channel into an irreversible 1-hour close countdown, enabling protocol-wide griefing DoS that pressures payees to settle all vouchers within the grace period or lose unsettled payments.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The initiateClose() function at TempoStreamChannel.sol lines 260-271 performs no msg.sender validation. It only checks that the channel exists (payer != address(0)), is not finalized, and that gracePeriodEnd == 0 (not already closing). Any external address can call this function on any active channel between any two parties. Once called, channel.gracePeriodEnd is set to block.timestamp + GRACE_PERIOD (1 hour) and there is no mechanism to cancel or reset this. After the grace period expires, finalize() — which also has no msg.sender check — can be called by anyone to close the channel and refund all unsettled deposit to the payer. This constitutes a clear missing access control defect: a state-changing function that should be restricted to channel participants (payer/payee) is callable by any address.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Lines 260-271 of TempoStreamChannel.sol show initiateClose with no msg.sender check. The function signature is `function initiateClose(bytes32 channelId) external` with no modifiers. The function body only validates channel existence, non-finalization, and that grace period hasn't started — never checks who is calling. Similarly, finalize() at lines 278-296 has no msg.sender check. There is no other function that can reset gracePeriodEnd to 0 once set.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No msg.sender validation. Any address can call this on any active channel, forcing it into an irreversible 1-hour grace period countdown. Should require msg.sender == channel.payer || msg.sender == channel.payee.
No msg.sender validation. After the grace period initiated by anyone via initiateClose, anyone can call finalize to close the channel and refund unsettled deposits to the payer.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker monitors ChannelOpened events to collect active channelIds
2. Attacker calls initiateClose(channelId) — no msg.sender check at line 260
3. Channel existence verified: channel.payer != address(0) at line 262
4. Channel not finalized: channel.finalized check at line 263
5. Grace period not already active: channel.gracePeriodEnd == 0 at line 264
6. Grace period set: channel.gracePeriodEnd = block.timestamp + GRACE_PERIOD at line 266
7. After 1 hour, attacker calls finalize(channelId) at line 278
8. Grace period elapsed check passes: block.timestamp >= channel.gracePeriodEnd at lines 283-284
10. payerRefund = channel.deposit - channel.settled at line 288 — all unsettled funds refunded to payer
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The complete attack path requires two transactions: initiateClose (line 260) to start the grace period, then finalize (line 278) after 1 hour. Neither function checks msg.sender. The grace period cannot be cancelled once started — there is no function in the contract that sets gracePeriodEnd back to 0. The only alternative is cooperative close() which requires payer + authorizedSigner signatures. The channel is irreversibly pushed toward finalization.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The vulnerability is inherent in the contract code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is fully validated: (1) channelIds are observable from ChannelOpened events emitted at openChannel (line 237). (2) initiateClose has no access control — verified at lines 260-271, no msg.sender comparison exists. (3) gracePeriodEnd cannot be reset — no function in the contract sets it back to 0 once set to a nonzero value. (4) finalize has no access control — verified at lines 278-296. (5) After finalize, payerRefund = deposit - settled is sent to payer, meaning all unsettled voucher value is lost to the payee. A bot could iterate over all active channels at roughly ~50K gas per initiateClose call, then finalize all after 1 hour.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any active payment channel can be force-closed by any external address. The payee has only 1 hour to settle all outstanding vouchers before unsettled funds are returned to the payer. In a coordinated attack on all channels simultaneously, payees may be unable to settle all vouchers in time, especially under network congestion. This constitutes a protocol-wide denial of service. The financial impact is the total unsettled voucher value across all active channels. Even if payees manage to settle in time, the forced closure disrupts ongoing service relationships and requires new channels to be opened.
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to initiateClose: require(msg.sender == channel.payer || msg.sender == channel.payee, 'UnauthorizedCaller')
Consider adding access control to finalize as well, or at minimum keeping it permissionless but restricting initiateClose
Consider adding a mechanism to cancel/extend the grace period with mutual agreement (e.g., both payer and payee signatures)
Consider allowing the payee to extend the grace period unilaterally if they can prove they have valid unsettled vouchers
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy TempoStreamChannel to a local fork. 2. Open a channel between payer Alice and payee Bob with a deposit of 1000 tokens. 3. From a third unrelated address (attacker), call initiateClose(channelId). 4. Verify the call succeeds and gracePeriodEnd is set. 5. Advance block.timestamp by 1 hour. 6. From the attacker address, call finalize(channelId). 7. Verify the channel is finalized and 1000 tokens are refunded to Alice (payer), with Bob (payee) receiving nothing despite having valid unsettled vouchers.
uncertainties: The design intent is ambiguous — it's possible the developers intentionally made initiateClose permissionless as a feature (e.g., for dispute resolution by third-party arbitrators), though this would be highly unusual and no documentation supports this interpretation
The practical impact depends on how quickly payees can respond to unexpected close initiations and settle their vouchers within the 1-hour window
additional_testing_needed: Foundry test confirming third-party can call initiateClose and finalize
Gas benchmarking for attacking N channels simultaneously
Integration test showing payee fund loss when unable to settle within grace period
